findings.explanation: `fromPairs` assigns attacker-controlled keys directly into a plain object, which can set special keys like `__proto__` and lead to prototype pollution.
`zipObject` uses `baseZipObject` with `assignValue`, allowing untrusted property names to be written into a plain object and potentially pollute prototypes.
`set` forwards arbitrary user-controlled paths to `baseSet`, which can create or modify nested properties from untrusted input and is a common prototype pollution sink if not constrained by callers.
`unset` accepts arbitrary paths and relies on `baseUnset`, which is a sensitive object-manipulation primitive that can be abused for prototype pollution in vulnerable call sites.
`merge` recursively copies inherited and own properties from attacker-controlled sources, making it a well-known prototype pollution sink when merging untrusted objects.
`mergeWith` exposes the same recursive merge behavior as `merge`, so untrusted source objects can inject special keys and pollute prototypes through custom merging logic.
`defaults` iterates over inherited properties and writes them into the destination object, which can allow special keys from untrusted input to alter object prototypes.

`defaultsDeep` delegates to deep merge logic on untrusted inputs, creating a classic prototype pollution primitive when attacker-controlled keys like `__proto__` or `constructor.prototype` are present.
`assignWith` copies source properties into the destination object using caller-supplied data, which can be abused with special keys to pollute prototypes.
`assignIn` copies inherited enumerable properties as well as own properties, increasing the risk of prototype pollution when merging attacker-controlled objects.
`assignInWith` performs inherited-property assignment with a customizer, which can propagate malicious keys into the target object and enable prototype pollution.
`defaults` writes missing inherited keys from source objects into the target, creating a prototype pollution risk when source data is attacker-controlled.
`template` compiles and executes arbitrary template code from the input string, so using untrusted templates can lead to script injection and XSS in web contexts.
Although `template` validates `variable` against some forbidden characters, the rest of the template string is still interpreted as code without adequate validation of untrusted input.
